summary: McKenzie presents a comprehensive exposé of Tether and Bitfinex as interconnected components of what he calls the largest fraud since Madoff. After Bitfinex lost banking access following a 2016 hack and Wells Fargo cutting ties, they intensified use of Tether as a pseudo-dollar system and routed funds through Crypto Capital Corp, a money launderer whose principal was skimming 10% of deposits. When CCC's accounts were frozen and funds embezzled, Tether became insolvent but survived through increasingly desperate measures including paying withdrawals with cryptocurrency, using employee friends' personal bank accounts as money mules, and raiding Bitfinex customer funds. The piece traces how each operational crisis necessitated a new layer of fraud, deepening the hole and creating systemic risk for the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem.
key_points: Tether was never reliably backed by USD reserves as claimed; its reserves were actually receivables from money launderers, related-party IOUs, and cryptocurrency, with the company admitting in court to approximately 74% backing at best

Crypto Capital Corp, Tether's primary banking partner, was a money launderer whose architect skimmed 10% of all deposits for personal use including funding a failed football league, while other funds were frozen by regulators

The fraud follows a recurring pattern where each operational crisis requires a new, deeper fraud to conceal the previous one — identical to the Mt. Gox collapse pattern McKenzie had previously documented

Tether represents systemic risk to cryptocurrency markets analogous to money market funds in traditional finance, functioning as critical load-bearing infrastructure whose failure could cascade through the entire ecosystem
McKenzie published SHA-hashed predictions to Twitter months before facts became public, demonstrating he had identified the insolvency and fraud mechanisms through independent analysis
money_quotes: Tether is the internal accounting system for the largest fraud since Madoff.
Every dollar that goes out to buy the clichéd Lambo is a dollar that went in from a speculator.
Some cryptocurrency exchanges have an attitude towards compliance which is best described as 'Bond villain.'
When your fraud has an operational hiccup, you have to fraud harder, or your fraud will be discovered!
A lot of people currently think they are richer than they are.

Bitfinex and its principals have not yet been indicted by the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York, but crucially, not in the same sense that you have not been indicted by the U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York.
The community extremely underestimates the degree to which the designed intent and best use case of Tether is facilitating money laundering.
I have looked, quite a bit. I have not found a good use case yet, or the revolutionary technology advances that my friends tell me exist, but I have found some frauds.
key_insight: Tether and Bitfinex form a self-reinforcing fraud where each crisis demands a deeper deception, creating systemic risk for the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem while the community collectively looks away because acknowledging the fraud would collapse the asset prices sustaining their businesses.
